chunk_summary: Adolescent PCOS diagnosis in the context of obesity requires age-appropriate criteria, metabolic risk evaluation, and structured lifestyle management, avoiding overdiagnosis and unnecessary tests like pelvic ultrasound or anti-Müllerian hormone levels.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific diagnostic criteria for adolescent PCOS, including menstrual irregularity and hyperandrogenism.
Identify metabolic risks associated with PCOS in obese adolescents and appropriate screening tests.
Learn first-line management strategies for adolescent PCOS, including lifestyle interventions and pharmacological options.
Recognize conditions mimicking PCOS and their exclusion criteria in clinical practice.
key_facts: Irregular cycles within the first year post-menarche are considered normal; PCOS diagnosis requires persistent irregularity and hyperandrogenism.
Pelvic ultrasound is not recommended for diagnosis within 8 years post-menarche; anti-Müllerian hormone is also not diagnostic in adolescence.
Metabolic screening includes BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, lipids, and ALT to assess risks like steatotic liver disease.
Structured lifestyle treatment and weight management are first-line interventions for adolescent PCOS.
Combined oral contraceptives are commonly used for menstrual regulation and hyperandrogenism symptoms, with contraindication screening.
reference_quote: Irregular cycles within the first year post-menarche can be normal; adolescent PCOS diagnosis requires both menstrual irregularity and clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, excluding mimicking disorders. (International evidence-based guideline for the assessment and management of PCOS, 2023)
document_key_topics: Metabolic risk screening in obesity clinics
Lifestyle and pharmacological management of PCOS
Exclusion of mimicking disorders in PCOS diagnosis
